results.title: Vicki's story
results.creator_credit: Vicki Atkinson and ACMI Digital Storytelling
results.brief_description: Vicki reminisces about her family and childhood and how she looked up to her parents as role models hoping that what she has learned from them can be passed on to her own two daughters. This is her message.

results.title: A Place called Gapuwiyak
results.creator_credit: Nicole La Gerche and ACMI Digital Storytelling
results.brief_description: This is Nicole’s story of her experiences living in East Arnhem, of being adopted into and accepted by the Wunungmurra family and the Yolngu community.

results.title: ASHE: the way of life
results.creator_credit: Colin Colger and ACMI Digital Storytelling
results.brief_description: After the death of his mother and losing his way to a life of crime, Colin, a sports loving Shepparton boy, was offered a new beginning - a lifeline - through ASHE (Academy of Health Sports and Education).

results.title: Baby girl
results.creator_credit: Chrissy Ponting and ACMI Digital Storytelling
results.brief_description: Life is very challenging for Chrissy as a teenage mum but through her love for daughter Lakeisha combined with the support of family and friends, she has been able to complete her school studies and begin training as a nurse. This is dedicated to her baby girl.

results.title: To my dearest beloved one - Shewitey Mearey
results.brief_description: A personal account created by a participant in the ACMI Digital Storytelling Workshop “Journeys with Breast Cancer” Created in association with Breast Cancer Network Australia. “I said to the doctor, I don’t care if I die tomorrow but I don’t want to leave my daughter alone. He said to me that first I had to live before I could have another child.”

results.title: Fair treatment
results.creator_credit: Tim Johnson, Chris McGill and Swinburne Institute of Technology. Film and Television School
results.brief_description: This program examines some of the most important issues facing people living with HIV and contains interviews with people who are HIV positive. It focuses on the availability of drugs for HIV patients, and the lack of action and apathy shown by Australian governments. It also explores the outlook on life of the interviewees, how the stigma of AIDS, the death of friends and loved ones has affected them and finally examines their attitudes to death and dying.
results.creator_credit_for_label: Tim Johnson, Chris McGill and Swinburne Institute of Technology. Film and Television School

results.title: Percy Grainger's MOOG Theremin
results.brief_description: Cinema audiences were first introduced to the eerie vibrations of a theremin in the Soviet film Odna (Alone) (1931). During a snowstorm, composer Dimitri Shostakovish amps up the dread with the theremin’s high-pitched wail. Invented in 1920 by Russian physicist Léon Theremin, the instrument works by harnessing electricity and has been used in movies ever since, from The Day the Earth Stood Still (1951) to First Man (2018). In 1932, Australian composer Percy Grainger discovered the instrument (this one is signed by him) and used it to develop compositions that weren’t dictated by scales, rhythms or harmonies.

constellations_primary.name: Good vibrations
constellations_primary.description: Since its invention, the humble theremin has been used in a number of different ways to evoke all sorts of strange sounds and moods.

group_works.title: Theremin: An Electronic Odyssey documentary poster

results.title: Theremin: An Electronic Odyssey documentary poster
results.brief_description: Poster for the documentary ‘Theremin: An Electronic Odyssey.’ This documentary explores the life of Leon Theremin, the inventor of an electronic synthesizer called the theremin, which uses motion to produce a sliding ethereal sound. After creating the instrument, Theremin is kidnapped by the KGB to work on inventions and surveillance technology. During his absence, the theremin becomes a popular instrument in film scores and through the performances of Clara Rockmore. In 1991, Theremin finally returns to the United States after his release.

details.label: Curatorial section
display_values: The Story of the Moving Image → Moving Pictures → MI-05. Sound and Colour → MI-05-C02

results.title: Electric sound
results.brief_description: The wave of electric sound from the 40s onwards transformed not just how people watched movies and how they identified genre, but how they listened to it as well. The otherworldly chords of the theremin became indelibly linked to science fiction.

constellations_primary.name: Electric sound supercut: composers and their distinctive other worlds
constellations_primary.description: Electric sound is commonly associated with science fiction narratives for its auditory oddness, which has symbolised other worldly environments, the unconscious and the 1980s.

results.description: Specific types of movies have a specific sound. Overtime, these associations inevitably become subconscious. The wave of electric sound from the 40s onwards transformed not just how people watched movies and how they identified genre, but how they listened to it as well. The otherworldly chords of the theremin became indelibly linked to science fiction, while husband and wife composers Bebe and Louis Barron are credited with creating the first fully realised electronic film score for 50s cult classic Forbidden Planet (1956). From deploying unconventional instruments to early looping and splicing techniques, the legacy of electric sound can still be found today in the works of composers like Danny Elfman.
description_for_label: Specific types of movies have a specific sound. Overtime, these associations inevitably become subconscious. The wave of electric sound from the 40s onwards transformed not just how people watched movies and how they identified genre, but how they listened to it as well. The otherworldly chords of the theremin became indelibly linked to science fiction, while husband and wife composers Bebe and Louis Barron are credited with creating the first fully realised electronic film score for 50s cult classic Forbidden Planet (1956). From deploying unconventional instruments to early looping and splicing techniques, the legacy of electric sound can still be found today in the works of composers like Danny Elfman.
